attributes.stem:
A 30-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a history of fainting. He describes three previous fainting episodes, which were sudden-onset, with associated dizziness prior to fainting. He also describes feeling nauseated and sweaty prior to each faint. Each episode has occurred while he has been at work as a paramedic at various jobs involving motor vehicle accidents, lasting between 30 and 45 seconds. He does not describe any incontinence or tongue-biting associated with the faints, and has always recovered immediately following the episodes. He has a medical history of type 1 diabetes mellitus, for which he takes regular basal-bolus insulin. His last HbA1c was 6.5%, and he states he has regular contact with his endocrinologist. He does not take any other regular medications. He has previously been involved as a victim in a car accident.

On examination, he appears well. His pulse rate is 78/min, blood pressure 115/74 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 96% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. The rest of the examination is unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is most suggestive of a vasovagal aetiology for his syncope?
options: Fainting upon arriving at the scene of a motor vehicle accident
Presence of severe structural heart disease
attributes.explanation:
This case presents a clear example of neurally-mediated syncope, most likely emotion-induced vasovagal syncope. The absence of classical signs of seizure (e.g., tongue biting, post-ictal phase) points to syncope as the most likely diagnosis. The patient's tendency toward fainting upon arriving at the scene of a motor vehicle accident is highly suggestive that his emotional reaction to this event is causing his faints. Other triggers for vasovagal syncope include pain and fear.

While the patient does have a history of type 1 diabetes mellitus, which may raise the possibility of autonomic neuropathy, this would be less likely given his excellent glycemic control (HbA1c 6.5%). Moreover, autonomic neuropathy typically causes orthostatic hypotension rather than situational syncope.

Fainting with arm exercise would be suggestive of exertional syncope, which can be associated with structural heart disease or arrhythmias. However, as this patient's syncopal episodes do not occur alongside physical exertion, this is unlikely to be correct.

An olfactory hallucination prodrome preceding a syncopal episode would be more in keeping with a seizure, rather than a syncopal event. The patient does not report any such symptoms, nor does he have risk factors such as a history of temporal lobe epilepsy, schizophrenia, or brain tumour.

The presence of severe structural heart disease can be associated with symptoms including chest pain, dyspnea, syncope, or palpitations. Conditions such as Hypertrophic Obstructive Cardiomyopathy (HOCM) can be detected through careful exploration of family history, physical examination (including eliciting outflow tract murmur on Valsalva/squatting), ECG criteria for left-ventricular hypertrophy, and echocardiographic evidence of structural abnormality. However, the patient's age, lack of cardiac symptoms, and normal physical examination make severe structural heart disease less likely.

In this case, the clear association between the syncopal events and emotionally charged situations (car crash scenes), along with the prodromal symptoms of nausea and sweating, strongly suggest vasovagal syncope. This is a common form of neurally-mediated syncope, where an emotional trigger leads to a sudden drop in heart rate and blood pressure, resulting in reduced cerebral perfusion and loss of consciousness.
learningPoints: Vasovagal syncope can be associated with strong emotional reactions.
Vasovagal syncope is a sub-type of neurally-mediated syncope.
Careful history-taking is crucial in differentiating causes of syncope.
references: Arthur W, Kaye GC. The pathophysiology of common causes of syncope. Postgraduate medical journal. 2000 Dec 1;76(902):750-3. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11085764/

Syncope Trust And Reflex anoxic Seizures. In: STARS Patient Information [online]. Cited 2022 Apr 15. Available at: https://www.heartrhythmalliance.org/stars/uk/reflex-syncope-vasovagal-syncope?gclid=Cj0KCQjw3IqSBhCoARIsAMBkTb1jnuEBvVcvB-DxctHvQ2LWj8BokFWEP1n2nOykJBjkpgWuaioq6
